title: Elevator Reader Count Guide
summary: Understand elevator access planning, cab readers, floor relays, schedules, and integration complexity.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps estimate elevator access-control complexity based on cab count, floor count, reader count, and floor-selection control requirements.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning elevator readers, destination dispatch coordination, floor lockout, tenant separation, or restricted-floor access.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Elevator Cabs
items.description: The number of elevator cars being controlled.
items.whyItMatters: More cabs increase reader and relay coordination.
items.commonMistake: Only counting floors and forgetting multiple cabs.

items.label: Controlled Floors
items.description: Floors that require access restriction or unlock control.
items.whyItMatters: More controlled floors usually require more relays or integration points.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every floor needs individual control when zones may be simpler.

items.label: Readers
items.description: Credential readers used in or near the elevator cab.
items.whyItMatters: Reader placement affects user flow and security behavior.
items.commonMistake: Placing readers without confirming elevator operating sequence.

items.label: Relay Outputs
items.description: Outputs used to enable floor buttons or elevator inputs.
items.whyItMatters: Relay count can grow quickly with floors and cabs.
items.commonMistake: Underestimating relay hardware and cabinet space.

items.label: Schedules
items.whyItMatters: Schedules can complicate troubleshooting and user expectations.
items.commonMistake: Using too many unique floor schedules.

items.label: Vendor Coordination
items.description: Coordination needed with elevator controls or destination dispatch.
items.whyItMatters: Elevators often require vendor-specific integration details.
items.commonMistake: Assuming access control can be added without elevator contractor involvement.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the elevator access model is likely straightforward. Watch means relay count, schedules, or integration should be reviewed. Risk means the elevator scope may need deeper coordination with the elevator vendor and access-control manufacturer.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: simplify the design, add capacity, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most access-control design problems come from underestimating how hardware, users, schedules, exceptions, and maintenance effort interact over time.
bullets: Designing only for the initial install instead of the system lifecycle.
Ignoring exceptions, expansion, or administrative workload.
Assuming manufacturer capacity limits are the same as practical design limits.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, code review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Local code and life-safety requirements still apply.
Site conditions may change the final design.
A simpler design is usually easier to support, audit, and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual product and platform being installed.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
